data.title: Baker Hughes to sell Waygate Technologies to Hexagon in $1.45B all-cash deal
data.description:
• Baker Hughes (BKR) has entered into an agreement to sell its Waygate Technologies business to Hexagon in an all-cash transaction valued at about $1.45B, the oilfield services firm said on Monday.
  • The sale encompasses Waygate Technologies’ remote visual inspection, ultrasound, radiography, and imaging solutions portfolios, and all assets of the business, including intellectual property, footprint and resources.
  • The divestiture, along with three completed deals and the pending purchase of Chart Industries, shows Baker Hughes’ focus on reshaping its portfolio. These actions are aimed at making earnings and cash flow more stable while also strengthening its balance sheet, the company said.
  • The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026. J.P. Morgan Securities LLC is serving as exclusive financial advisor for Baker Hughes on this transaction.

data.title: Gold hits weekly low on geopolitical friction, but Swiss bank bets on $6,000 moonshot
data.description:
Gold prices fell to a near one-week low on Monday, pressured by a stronger dollar, while a sharp jump in oil prices after failed U.S.-Iran peace talks stoked inflation concerns and reduced expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) eased 0.6% to $4,725.29 an ounce, after rising about 2% in the previous week.

Elsewhere, Union Bancaire Privée is buying gold again after cutting a significant position in response to an Iran war-induced slump, saying it believes the long-term outlook remains intact, Bloomberg reported.

The Swiss private bank is gradually adding bullion to discretionary client portfolios after cutting exposure to 3% from around 10%. The metal has tumbled since the war began due to fears of higher interest rates and a liquidity squeeze that saw traders offload holdings to cover losses elsewhere.

The dollar (DXY) gained ⁠0.4%, while oil prices  bounced back above $100 a barrel, as the U.S. Navy prepared ​a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz that could restrict Iranian oil shipments, following the U.S. ​and Iran's failure to reach a deal to end the war.

"Ceasefire ​optimism has unwound following the failure of the peace talks, and the resulting push ​higher by the dollar and oil prices has put gold on the back foot again," Tim ‌Waterer, ⁠chief market analyst at KCM Trade told Reuters.

Meanwhile the World Gold Council recently said that gold market liquidity stayed strong despite the March sell-off, with trading volumes remaining elevated across major segments.

Big players like ANZ and Goldman Sachs are also staying positive about gold's future.

data.title: Kornit Digital buys PrintFactory to strengthen automation capabilities
data.description:
• Kornit Digital (KRNT) acquired PrintFactory, a Netherlands-based tech company providing cloud-native workflow, color management, and production automation software.
  • The acquisition supports Kornit’s (KRNT) strategic shift toward consultative, solution-oriented engagement with customers across the production value chain.
  • The transaction is slated to close in Q2 2026.
  • The financial terms of the deal were not disclosed, and Kornit (KRNT) stated the acquisition is not expected to have a material impact on near‑term revenue or earnings.
  • “This acquisition is about more than workflow or color management,” said Ronen Samuel, CEO of Kornit Digital. “It is about building the digital infrastructure the fashion industry needs to move from analog production to agile, on-demand manufacturing. By connecting demand generation, production workflow and manufacturing through one integrated platform, we are accelerating the industry’s transition to a new production model.”

data.title: Trump issues 50% China tariff ultimatum over Iran aid as U.S. Navy begins Strait of Hormuz blockade
data.description:
President Donald Trump escalated geopolitical tensions on Sunday, warning Beijing that the U.S. would impose "staggering" 50% tariffs on Chinese goods if Beijing provides military aid to Iran during the current U.S.-Israeli conflict.

Speaking with Maria Bartiromo on Fox News, Trump characterized the potential penalties as a direct consequence of any support for Tehran. 

Trump reinforced the ultimatum on Truth Social, writing, "A country supplying military weapons to Iran will be immediately tariffed, on any and all goods sold to the United States of America, 50%, effective immediately... There will be no exclusions or exemptions!"

However, according to a Reuters report noted that China has officially denied allegations of recent arms shipments.

Despite the escalation, a high-stakes summit between Trump and President Xi Jinping remains scheduled for next month in Beijing.

Earlier on Sunday, Trump ordered a U.S. naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz following the collapse of peace talks in Islamabad. He noted that while negotiations initially showed promise, Iran remained firm on its nuclear ambitions and refused to keep the strait open—which is vital for global oil transportation. 

Trump stated, "Effective immediately, the United States Navy will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz."

Trump stated via social media, adding a stern warning: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"

While the President initially suggested broader international involvement, he later confirmed that Britain and several other allies would specifically deploy minesweepers to support the effort.

The threat of massive tariffs on China and the oil blockade in the Middle East are fueling fears of a global economic slowdown, sending oil prices sharply higher.

data.title: MDA Space launches MIDNIGHT platform to boost orbital defense capabilities
data.description:
• MDA Space (MDA) unveiled MDA MIDNIGHT, a maneuverable space control platform designed for defense organizations to defend and protect the space domain.
  • The platform, introduced at the Space Symposium in Colorado Springs, is built as a “space domain bodyguard” system, capable of protecting satellites and maintaining operational continuity in increasingly contested orbital environments.
  • MDA (MDA) said the system is designed as a mission-ready, rapidly deployable solution helping the company tap into growing defense demand for protecting national space assets and orbital security.

data.title: Sanofi, Regeneron’s Dupixent approved in EU for young children with chronic urticaria
data.description:
• Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron’s (REGN) Dupixent approved in EU for treating moderate-to-severe chronic spontaneous urticaria ((CSU)) in children aged two to 11 years, according to a statement on Monday.
  • Approval in CSU for children 2 to 11 years of age is based on data from the LIBERTY-CUPID clinical trial program, including an extrapolation of efficacy data showing that Dupixent significantly reduced urticaria activity compared with placebo in adults.
  • The latest approval expands Dupixent’s indication for CSU in the EU to children as young as 2 years; Dupixent is now approved for children less than 12 years of age across four chronic diseases driven in part by type 2 inflammation, the companies said.
  • In the U.S., the supplemental Biologics License Application (sBLA) for Dupixent has been accepted for review in certain children aged 2 to 11 years with CSU. Dupixent is currently approved for CSU in certain adults and adolescents in many jurisdictions, including the U.S. and Japan.

data.title: Chinese rare earth producers hike prices by 44% for Q2
data.description:
Chinese rare earth producers' shares moved higher on Monday after key producers announced a sharp price hike in rare earth products for Q2, underscoring tightening supply and Beijing’s continued grip on the strategic metals.

China Northern Rare Earth and Baotou Steel raised the related‑party transaction price for rare‑earth concentrate to 38,804 yuan per metric ton for Q2 2026, up roughly 45% from 26,834 yuan in Q1. The move marks one of the steepest increases in recent quarters, with prices climbing multiple consecutive quarters amid tightening supply-demand dynamics. 

China continues to dominate global rare earth supply chains, accounting for the majority of refining and processing capacity. The pricing is often guided by structured mechanisms and state coordination rather than fully open-market dynamics.

The demand remains robust for EVs, wind turbines, consumer electronics, and defense applications, and global buyers have stepped up restocking amid export restriction fears and regional geopolitical tensions.

The stock price reaction was immediate in related names: Baotou Steel surged as much as 8.5%, China Northern Rare Earth rose about 3%, and China Rare Earth Resources & Technology gained around 2.5%.

data.title: Meatpacker JBS reaches labor deal with striking workers at Colorado plant
data.description:
• Workers at one of the nation’s largest meatpacking plants who staged a multiweek strike have reached an agreement with plant owner JBS USA, the company and labor union representatives announced Sunday.
  • The Swift Beef Co. plant in Greeley, Colorado, will immediately return to normal operations after weeks of uncertainty, JBS USA said in a statement, according to an AP report.
  • The agreement comes after thousands of workers at the meat processing plant led a three-week strike with the United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 Union in a bid for higher wages and better health care. The strike ended April 4 after JBS USA agreed to resume negotiations.
  • Workers and JBS USA agreed to wage increases over the next two years and a $750 one-time bonus. The tentative agreement represents a contract with “all gains, countless improvements, and not a single concession,” the union said.

data.description:

Existing home sales tally the number of previously constructed homes, condominiums, and co-ops in which a sale closed during the month.

data.title: Asia stocks retreat as Hormuz Blockade fears and stalled peace talks weigh on sentiment; oil prices spike
data.description:
Asian equity markets fell on Monday after US-Iran peace talks ended without progress, raising global growth concerns. Trump announced on Sunday that the US Navy would enforce a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, increasing tensions and oil prices.

Gold prices dropped about 2% to below $4,700 an ounce on Monday.

WTI crude futures climbed as much as 9.3% to above $105 per barrel on Monday.

The benchmark KOSPI fell nearly 1% to around 5,805 on Monday, reversing from the previous session. The Bank of Korea 

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.94% to below 56,500 on Monday, giving back part of last week’s gains. The Japanese yen depreciated past 159.5 per dollar on Monday, sliding for the third straight session.

China's (SHCOMP) fell 0.17% to 3,980 on Monday, while the Shenzhen Component rose 0.5% to 14,386, and the offshore yuan weakened to around 6.83 per dollar on Monday.

US President Donald Trump recently warned that countries aiding Iran's military, including China, could face economic penalties, potentially leading to tariffs of up to 50% on exports to the US.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.15% to 25,600 on Monday, reversing gains from last week’s rally.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.76% to 76,007 due to higher oil prices, and the rupee fell to around 93.37 per dollar. The Asian Development Bank raised India's GDP growth forecast for FY2026-27 to 6.9% from 6.5%.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.51% to 8,907 in early deals on Monday, marking losses for the second straight session. The Australian dollar weakened to below $0.703, easing from a three-week high.

In the U.S. on Friday, all three major indexes struggled amid seemingly lower geopolitical tensions.

U.S. stock futures declined on Monday after President Donald Trump announced a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz: Dow -0.79%; S&P 500 -0.76%; Nasdaq -0.85%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).
